course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
title: Double Integrals, Change of Variables, and Green's Theorem
sessionGoal: Students should be able to set up double integrals, use polar coordinates when helpful, and apply Green's Theorem to convert a line integral to a double integral.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start with the sentence: always draw the region first.
Do Problem 1 to practice switching order.
Do Problem 2 to practice polar coordinates.
Do Problem 3 to practice Green's Theorem and orientation.
sayThisFirst: For double integrals, the region matters as much as the function.
When switching order, redraw the region and rewrite the bounds.
Use polar when the region has circles, disks, or angles.
Green's Theorem changes a closed line integral into a double integral over the region inside.
boardPlan: Write 'draw first' at the top of the board.
Do one polar integral over a disk/half-disk.
Do one Green's Theorem problem over a rectangle.
keyFormulas: Green's Theorem: \(\oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy=\iint_D(Q_x-P_y)\,dA\), for positive orientation.
For switching order: describe the same region using the other variable as the outside bound.
commonMistakes: Not drawing the region before changing bounds.
Using Green's Theorem when the curve is not closed.
Forgetting that clockwise orientation changes the sign.
notationWarnings: Use \(D\) for the region and \(C=\partial D\) for its boundary.
For Green's Theorem, clearly identify \(P\) and \(Q\).
Write 'CCW' or 'clockwise' near the boundary arrows.

problems.title: Switch order and evaluate
problems.studentPromptLatex: Rewrite the integral with the order reversed, then evaluate: \[\int_0^2\int_x^2 (x+y)\,dy\,dx.\]
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: read the bounds as inequalities, then draw the region.

visualSpec.regionDescription: Triangle bounded by y=x, y=2, and x=0.
drawInstructions: Draw the triangle bounded by x=0, y=x, and y=2.
Show vertical slices for the original order.
Show horizontal slices for the reversed order.

walkthrough.label: Describe the region
walkthrough.latex: The original bounds say \[0\le x\le2,\qquad x\le y\le2.\] This is the triangle above \(y=x\), below \(y=2\), and to the right of \(x=0\).

walkthrough.label: Reverse the order
walkthrough.latex: Using horizontal slices, \(y\) goes from \(0\) to \(2\). For a fixed \(y\), \(x\) goes from \(0\) to \(y\). So \[\int_0^2\int_x^2 (x+y)\,dy\,dx=\int_0^2\int_0^y (x+y)\,dx\,dy.\]

commonMistakes: Forgetting that the lower y-value is 0 in the reversed order.
Changing the region while changing the order.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 9 for double integrals and changing order.

problems.title: Polar integral over a half disk
problems.studentPromptLatex: Evaluate \(\iint_D (x^2+y^2)\,dA\), where \(D\) is the upper half disk \(x^2+y^2\le4\), \(y\ge0\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: circles should make you think polar. Also, never forget the extra r in dA.

drawInstructions: Draw the upper half disk of radius 2.
Write x^2+y^2=r^2 and dA=r dr dtheta.

walkthrough.label: Convert the region
walkthrough.latex: For the upper half disk of radius 2, \[0\le r\le2,\qquad 0\le\theta\le\pi.\]

walkthrough.label: Convert the integrand and area element

commonMistakes: Using \(0\le\theta\le2\pi\) for the upper half disk.
Leaving the integrand as \(x^2+y^2\) after switching to polar.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 9 for polar coordinates and change of variables.

problems.title: Green's Theorem over a rectangle
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(C\) be the positively oriented boundary of the rectangle \(0\le x\le3\), \(0\le y\le2\). Use Green's Theorem to compute \[\oint_C (x-y^2)\,dx+(2xy)\,dy.\]
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: positively oriented means counterclockwise. Green's Theorem turns the boundary integral into an area integral.

drawInstructions: Put counterclockwise arrows on the boundary.

commonMistakes: Forgetting that positive orientation is counterclockwise.
Trying to compute the four boundary integrals directly when Green's Theorem is faster.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 9 for Green's Theorem.

wrapUpQuestions: What is the first thing you should do for any double integral region?
When should polar coordinates be considered?
What are the two signs to be careful with in Green's Theorem?
qualityControlNotes: Checked Green's Theorem integrand 4y and value 24.
Visual specs include exact regions and orientation arrows.
reviewSummary: Session 21 is ready with checked double-integral, polar, and Green's Theorem problems.
